issue_title: libgdynet linked to libdynet (causing double memory free)
issue_body:
This fix introduced a tricky error: https://github.com/clab/dynet/commit/06b1dd1b97f30feda05284d94554743eb058a286

Basically, `libdynet` should implement everything only on CPU, while `libgdynet` implements things compiled on CPU+GPU, and they are independent of each-other. (Once multi-device support is working properly, `libdynet` will be phased out, and only `libgdynet` will remain, with the option to choose CPU or GPU).

This fix inadvertently broke this independence, and now `libdynet` is linked by `libgdynet`. This is resulting in memory errors at the end of program execution because both libraries are trying to free the same memory. Normally this would be simple, but on mac OS it was a bit complicated because of a circular dependency between `libgdynet` (which implements main functionality) and `libdynetcuda` (which implements the GPU kernels).

As a proposed solution, I'd like to try to get rid of the `libdynetcuda` library, which contains only GPU code, and combine this with `libgdynet`. I've started doing this here, but it's still giving linker errors on boost: https://github.com/clab/dynet/tree/remove-dynetcuda
I may not have time to actually implement this today, so I'll post this here for now to keep people informed and allow anyone who's interested to take a look.

I posted a question here: https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/dynet-users/uaJajWicH9g 

It's about this weird issue I'm having with my saved parameter values not matching those that I see when I call `dy.parameter(parameter_X).npvalue().

What's even weirder is when I built a simple, dummy example to accompany this issue, the problem did *not* occur.

Anyway, here's the toy example I built:

```
import dynet_config
import dynet as dy
import numpy as np

model = dy.ParameterCollection()

builders = [
    dy.GRUBuilder(1, 10, 7, model),
    dy.GRUBuilder(1, 10, 7, model)
]

pV = model.add_parameters((14, 2))
pb = model.add_parameters(2)

E = model.add_lookup_parameters((12, 8))
ET = model.add_lookup_parameters((7, 2))

trainer = dy.AdamTrainer(model)

dy.renew_cg()
# value of V before any training
V_original = dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()

# save without training
dy.save(
	"/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_without_training",
	[E, ET] + builders + [pV, pb]
)

# load and compare
loaded_model_without_training = dy.ParameterCollection()
loaded_params_without_training = dy.load(
	"/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_without_training",
	loaded_model_without_training
)

V_loaded_without_training = dy.parameter(loaded_params_without_training[4]).npvalue()

# compare V_original and V_loaded
print("V_original == V_loaded_without_training?")
print(np.all(V_original == V_loaded_without_training))

# train
for i in range(1000):
    dy.renew_cg()
    for datapoint, tags, labels in [
    ([1,2,3], [1, 1, 1], [0,0,1]),
    ([0,3,4], [2, 2, 2], [0,0,1])
    ]:
        V = dy.parameter(pV)
        b = dy.parameter(pb)
        words_in = [E[wid] for wid in datapoint]
        tags_in = [ET[tid] for tid in tags]
        init_states = builders[0].initial_state()
        init_states_b = builders[1].initial_state()
        in_ = [dy.concatenate([w, t]) for w,t in zip(words_in, tags_in)]
        in_b = [dy.concatenate([ww, tt]) for ww,tt in zip(reversed(words_in), reversed(tags_in))]
        hidden = [x.output() for x in init_states.add_inputs(in_)]
        hidden_b = [x.output() for x in init_states_b.add_inputs(in_b)]
        hidden_c = [dy.concatenate([h, hb]) for h, hb in zip(hidden, hidden_b)]
        outs = [dy.transpose(dy.transpose(hc) * V) + b for hc in hidden_c]
        losses = [dy.pickneglogsoftmax(o,l) for o,l in zip(outs, labels)]
        losses_summed = dy.esum(losses)
        losses_summed.backward()
        trainer.update()

dy.renew_cg()
V_after_training = dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()

print("testing")
dy.renew_cg()
for datapoint, tags, labels in [
    ([1,2,3], [1, 1, 1], [0,0,1]),
    ([0,3,4], [2, 2, 2], [0,0,1])
    ]:
    V = dy.parameter(pV)
    b = dy.parameter(pb)
    words_in = [E[wid] for wid in datapoint]
    tags_in = [ET[tid] for tid in tags]
    init_states = builders[0].initial_state()
    init_states_b = builders[1].initial_state()
    in_ = [dy.concatenate([w, t]) for w,t in zip(words_in, tags_in)]
    in_b = [dy.concatenate([ww, tt]) for ww,tt in zip(reversed(words_in), reversed(tags_in))]
    hidden = [x.output() for x in init_states.add_inputs(in_)]
    hidden_b = [x.output() for x in init_states_b.add_inputs(in_b)]
    hidden_c = [dy.concatenate([h, hb]) for h, hb in zip(hidden, hidden_b)]
    outs = [dy.transpose(dy.transpose(hc) * V) + b for hc in hidden_c]
    print(outs)


dy.renew_cg()
V_after_testing = dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()

dy.save(
    "/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_after_training",
	[E, ET] + builders + [pV, pb]
)

loaded_model_after_training = dy.ParameterCollection()

loaded_params_after_training = dy.load(
    "/home/mcapizzi/Desktop/test_model_after_training",
    loaded_model_after_training
)

# load V
dy.renew_cg()
V_loaded_after_training = dy.parameter(loaded_params_after_training[4]).npvalue()

print("V_loaded")
print(V_loaded_after_training)
print("V_after_testing")
print(V_after_testing)
print("V_after_training")
print(V_after_training)
```

But as I said, in this case the parameter values for `V` when loaded *do exactly* match those I see from `dy.parameter(pV).npvalue()`.  

My script that is having the problem is incredibly long and hard to follow, and so I do not want to subject you all to that.  But Graham suggested opening an issue nonetheless.

Thanks in advance for any advice, and for continuing to develop this great package.
-M

issue_title: How to handle an optional package dependency in tests?
issue_body:
#1691 adds `gcloud.monitoring` for accessing the Google Monitoring API. The initial focus is on querying of metric data.

The primary way in which users will want to get metric data is as a [pandas](http://pandas.pydata.org/pandas-docs/stable/) `DataFrame`, and the `Query` class offers an `as_dataframe()` method for this purpose.

However, `pandas` is a large package with dependencies on yet other packages. I would not recommend `gcloud-python` taking a dependency on it.

We have taken the approach of importing `pandas` dynamically in `as_dataframe()`. The documentation explains that use of this method requires that you have `pandas` installed. This works perfectly.

Tests are still an issue, however. The unit tests for `as_dataframe()` naturally require `pandas`, which is not currently present in the test environment.

How should we handle this?

issue_title: Docs lack guidance about choosing job_name parameter
issue_body:
Many methods on `google.cloud.bigquery.client.Client` take a `job_name` as their parameter but there's no guidance [in the docs](https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/client.html) about what the `job_name` should be.

Investigation suggests it needs to be unique, and this is confirmed under the "Generating a job ID" section of [this page](https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/managing-jobs) (which I found through Googling).  That page also suggests using eg Python's `uuid.uuid4()`.

Could some advice about choosing `job_name` be added to https://googlecloudplatform.github.io/google-cloud-python/latest/bigquery/client.html?

And better, although it'd be a backwards-incompatible change, could `job_name` be an optional parameter that's generated automatically by BQ?

issue_title: New Standard SQL Data Types Not Supported in gcloud._helpers._rows_from_json() / _CELLDATA_FROM_JSON()
issue_body:
Standard SQL dialect support was added in 0.18.0, but current release does not support queries that return results that include data types that are not supported by Legacy SQL.

E.g. executing the following query using Standard SQL:

```
SELECT PARSE_DATE('%Y%m%d', '20160810') AS table_date;
```

results in 

```
rows = query.rows
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gcloud/bigquery/query.py", line 204, in rows
    return _rows_from_json(self._properties.get('rows', ()), self.schema)
  File "/Library/Frameworks/Python.framework/Versions/2.7/lib/python2.7/site-packages/gcloud/bigquery/_helpers.py", line 85, in _rows_
from_json
    converter = _CELLDATA_FROM_JSON[field.field_type]
KeyError: u'DATE'
```

Not a significant issue since the Legacy SQL Data types will work for my needs, but wanted to report it all the same.

issue_title: Relation between account_daemon and oslogin tools
issue_body:
I am using [NixOS](https://nixos.org/) on GCE and am looking into integrating authorization/authentication features. I see a certain redundancy in the provided features and I would like to confirm that my conceptual understanding is correct. Could you please tell me if I understand it correctly that

1. `google_authorized_keys` executable, `pam_oslogin_{login,admin}.so` modules and `nss_oslogin` modules always query the metadata server and do not rely in any way on `google_accounts_daemon`.
2. the primary goal of `google_accounts_daemon` is to keep the system database of users/accounts (`/etc/passwd`) in sync with the metadata server (rather than to merely keep a local copy of user/account/rights from the metadata server).

I see that the provided set of tools provides a lot flexibility. What I do not quite understand is the motivation to duplicate/maintain GCE user accounts in the standard UNIX user/group database. The alternative I have in mind is that the GCE daemon
1. maintains GCE accounts in sync with the metadata server
2. exposes them via PAM modules which consumers are configured to use

With this design GCE and system accounts never mix so that if something fails it is easier to identify what went wrong. The priority of different authentication methods also becomes more transparent as it is explicit in the configuration files of PAM consumers.

One big difference in comparison with the existing design (if I understand it correctly) is that deployment would require to reconfigure all the relevant PAM consumers to use GCE modules. I don't see it as disadvantage as the resulting permissions would arguably become much more transparent to the user.

issue_title: Sass precision (default = 5) too low for $line-height-base calculation
issue_body:
in _variables.scss $line-height-base is set to 1.428571429
Sass cuts this to 1.42857. This leads to different heights in button and text Inputs. Text inputs are 1px higher. (osx, chrome)

Maybe there should be a note about the precision setting in sass to handle this issue.

issue_title: Cleanup ScroogeGen task - collapse from the Compiler abstraction
issue_body: We used to support 2 incompatible versions of the scrooge compiler but no longer do.  As a result the ScroogeGen task can be simplified by collapsing the Compiler abstraction and inlining functions and values and generally getting rid of now unused genericity.

It seems that the current beam search implementation (`_beam_search`) does not compute hypotheses length correctly. This can be seen when printing `sequences` and `lengths` just before returning from `_beam_search()`: the length is always one short of the actual non-zero values in sequences:

```
> sequences[0]
[    2    92  7741   907  2710   295    28     7  7741   907  2710     4
     7   383 10174  7324  9735   286   671   564  4085    71    22    98
 10671     4    13   782   485     8  6323  8665     4     8    23     8
  2976  1037  2448  3577    10   294   575  4664    13    34  3898   608
    43     5     3     0]
<NDArray 52 @cpu(0)>

> lengths[0]
[50.]
<NDArray 1 @cpu(0)>
```
`3` is the EOS id and the length of this hypothesis is clearly 51.

Let me try to explain the problem below. In practice, it doesn't matter since `_assemble_translation()` uses the length value to cut off the EOS symbol; the following EOS stripping logic in `_make_result()` is then redundant. But of course it shouldn't be and I think `_beam_search()` should return consistent data structures.

Here's the issue; I think this was introduced with length normalization only done for finished hypotheses:
1. `lengths` is initialized with 1s to account for the initial data in sequences, namely the BOS symbol
2. In each beam search iteration: as soon as we run topk on hypotheses scores, we effectively have expanded an hypothesis and its length increases by 1.
3. However, we are not using the updated length to potentially normalize the hypothesis if its finished. We check the newly acquired best_word_indices for EOS/PAD to compute a current `finished` status, and if so, **we normalize with the OLD length**.
4. This doesn't yet explain why we end beam_search with a length off by 1. This must somehow happen because the `length` array is only updated for hypotheses that are not finished and active. Since `finished` was just recomputed in step 3, the problem must lie in the `inactive` logic or the fact that right after we update `lengths`, we recompute `finished` once again and determine breaking out of the beam search loop based on that.

I am not 100% sure whats wrong here, probably the order of operations isn't entirely correct, but I also don't fully understand the whole `inactive` logic introduced. @mjpost could you comment on this?

At a minimum, we should make sure to normalize with correct length values. At best, we should make sure to return a `lengths` array from `_beam_search` that is consistent with `sequences`. With that, `_assemble_translation` would not silently cut off the last token (EOS), which by design should be handled later by `_make_result()`.

issue_title: Specifying RestApiId for EventSource will not update Lambda policy to let RestApiId Gateway invoke Lambda
issue_body:
Flow to reproduce
## Using SAM create Lambda and add API Gateway Event to trigger it 

```
GetHelloWorld:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: index.get
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Role: LambdaExecutionRole
      Events:
        GetEvent:
          Type: Api
          Properties:
            Path: /test
            Method: get
```
Expected result: 
1. Lambda is created
2. API Gateway (InitialAPIGateway) is created
3. "/test" endpoint is created
4. InitialAPIGateway is granted permission to invoke lambda

## Next step - we want to add swagger based API Gateway (to add CORS).

API Gateway Resource is added to SAM
```
  ApiGatewayApi:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Api
    Properties:
      StageName: Prod
      DefinitionBody:
        'Fn::Transform':
          Name: 'AWS::Include'
          Parameters:
            Location: s3://<bucket_name>/swagger.yaml

```
Swagger file  similar to one in examples folder is uploaded to bucket

Lambda is updated to reference created API Gateway
```
GetHelloWorld:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      Handler: index.get
      Runtime: nodejs4.3
      Role: LambdaExecutionRole
      Events:
        GetEvent:
          Type: Api
          ---->>>RestApiId: !Ref ApiGatewayApi
          Properties:
            Path: /test
            Method: get
```

Expected result:
1. Lambda is updated
2. API Gateway (SwaggerBasedGateway) is created
3. "/test" endpoint is created in SwaggerBasedGateway
4. SwaggerBasedGateway is granted permission to invoke lambda

Unexpected behavior:
1. Two API Gateways is created - InitialGateway and SwaggerBasedGateway
2. Lambda policy has rules for invocation by InitialGateway but has no rules for invocation by SwaggerBasedGateway. Lambda policy is not updated to allow invocation from Swagger generated api.

In [other issue](https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/issues/59#issuecomment-348709355) it was mentioned 
> If you are using separate Swagger, make sure you add the APIs to the events section as well. This will make SAM add correct Lambda::Permissions. See https://github.com/awslabs/serverless-application-model/blob/master/examples/2016-10-31/api_swagger_cors/template.yaml#L24-L35

This doesn't seem to work, as after referencing API in event, Lambda permission is not updated to be invoked from new API.
 
```
Events:
        GetEvent:
          Type: Api
          RestApiId: !Ref ApiGatewayApi
```

Admitting this is not known issue when Lambda can not be invoked from Gateway API Console.
In this case Curl gives the same result as function policy is not updated.
Additionaly I am not sure if this use-case should result in deploying 2 Gateway APIs once I reference RestApiId created from Swagger in event source.

issue_body:
I am getting an error when I try and export the `StandardScaler` transformer through a `PMMLPipeline`.  Here is the stack trace:

```
SEVERE: Failed to convert
java.lang.ClassCastException: sklearn.preprocessing.StandardScaler cannot be cast to sklearn.HasNumberOfFeatures
	at sklearn2pmml.PMMLPipeline.initFeatures(PMMLPipeline.java:153)

	at org.jpmml.sklearn.Main.run(Main.java:144)
	at org.jpmml.sklearn.Main.main(Main.java:93)
```

Below is the code that reproduces this error.

```python
import numpy as np
from sklearn import datasets, svm
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler
from sklearn2pmml import sklearn2pmml
from sklearn2pmml import PMMLPipeline

iris = datasets.load_iris()
X = iris.data
y = iris.target

X = X[y != 0, :2]
y = y[y != 0]
y = y.astype(np.float)

feat_normalizer = StandardScaler()
clf = svm.SVC(kernel='rbf', gamma=10)

sklearn2pmml(clf_module, "classifier.pmml", with_repr=True)
```

Any thoughts on what might be going on here?

issue_title: Missing support for microsecond precision in ORM DateTimeField
issue_body:
Micro precision support is disabled in the ```DatabaseFeatures``` class in ```base.py```

```
supports_microsecond_precision = False
```
See here: https://github.com/lionheart/django-pyodbc/blob/ba2daee41d222a76b4459733a5f6098f0098ab6b/django_pyodbc/base.py#L57

You can use the following as a workaround (place this e.g. in local_settings.py):
```
from django_pyodbc.base import DatabaseFeatures
DatabaseFeatures.supports_microsecond_precision = True
```

In #105 we describe basic Memento support with a deliverable being a TimeMap payload. For reasons of extensibility, we opted for a CDXJ TimeMap. This is due to the extensibility of the JSON field for each memento/entry, necessary for the IPFS hashes used to retrieve the content by IPWB.

The JSON format should be able to accommodate this format as well but neither JSON nor the more standard Link-formatted TimeMaps have a standard way to represent this necessary information. Supporting Link formatted TimeMaps will make ipwb more interoperable with other Memento tools.

[MemGator](https://github.com/oduwsdl/memgator) writes TimeMaps of all three of these formats, so its output can be used as a model.

issue_title: Combined naming bug with on-demand courses
issue_body:
I have tried the --combined-section-lectures-nums option with two on-demand courses, and for both I end up with several files beginning with "01_01". This is a big problem for me, as it means my phone will not list the videos in right order.

It seems the problem is week numbers are ignored and files are named by LL_VV_nameofvideo where LL=Lesson number and VV=Video number. If files could be prepended with week number the problem would be solved (named WW_LL_VV_nameofvideo where WW=Week number).

Further to this, in my opinion, the combined naming scheme should be the default - it adds very little length to the filename. I haven't been able to test with regular non-on-demand courses yet, so don't know if this is isolated to only on-demand courses or a general problem.

issue_title: Multiple threads being used by asyncio ApplicationRunner?!?!?!
issue_body:
Hi guys, I'm working with some Python 3 components that use asyncio and are run as guest workers in my Crossbar controller.

I've been having some weird issues lately that *seem* as if they could be threading-related deadlocks. Initially I was like, _that can't be happening, asyncio is single-threaded right?_ but when I ran `htop` and filtered the view to show one of my components it seems to tell a different story.

Here is a screenshot with `uvloop` powering things:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/248980/27173170-fead6452-51b7-11e7-8ae5-0d71d5102e02.png)

and here is another with the stock `asyncio` loop:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/248980/27173197-1c6e8502-51b8-11e7-8d34-b94268840a28.png)

and another with the stock `asyncio` loop after hitting a RPC end-point hosted by this component:
![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/248980/27173243-498e0594-51b8-11e7-8b7b-5087ea2298d2.png)

as you can see, `htop` indicates that threads are in use. There are differences between `uvloop` (which seems to use a fixed pool of 4 threads) and the stock loop (which starts with 1 thread and then scales up when it handles an RPC call) but overall the story is the same: threads are in use.

Is this normal? Is there something wrong with my components? Could this cause deadlock issues with my component (which is using a aiomysql connection pool attacked via a decorator to the RPC function being called)?

The current `DatasetReader.read` interface uses the same processing for training and validation/test data. It's possible that evaluation data might require a different kind of processing. For example, Chris' multi-paragraph model uses more paragraphs at test time compared to training time. 

One way to fix this would be to add a `evaluation` flag to read which would allow a different kind of processing. This would also be probably useful for `make-vocab` although you could simply create a new dataset reader for that.

Hello,
in the tarball released on PyPI, there are several pyc files - could you remove them from the next released tarball?

thanks!!

issue_title: Diamond does not set groups properly when changing uid and gid
issue_body:
According to (Python issue 10032)[http://bugs.python.org/issue10032], setting the `uid` and `gid` is not enough to simulate logging in as that user. The `os.initgroups()` function needs to be used to initialize the groups of the user being changed to, like this:

```python
    if gid != -1 and uid != -1:
        os.initgroups(pwd.getpwuid(uid).pw_name, gid)
```

If this is not done, Diamond can't invoke actions on files when it changes to a user that only has group permissions for those files, since Python doesn't know that it's a member of those groups.

While trying out pims `play` function in a notebook I got this error:
`ValueError: unknown encoding codec: 'libvpx'`

I know that this has something to do with my install of ffmpeg (2.2.4 on Ubuntu 12.04) but I can't figure out how to fix the weird dependency tangle. I found this: https://github.com/mikeboers/PyAV/issues/57 but even the recipe by @mkassner didn't fix this. I also tried some of the recipes here: https://github.com/conda-forge/staged-recipes/issues/708

This test https://github.com/soft-matter/pims/pull/71 worked?

Specify minimum versions in `setup.py`. This came up because `np.meshgrid` used to be 2D only, so there is some minimum version of numpy before which our ND functionality fails (and fails cryptically).

I'm not sure what the minimum version is, but most projects don't support numpy before 1.7.

issue_title: [discuss] Why we don't support ODS and DataPackages by default?
issue_body:
By default, tabulator doesn't support ODS and DataPackages, but supports other arguably less useful formats like `json`, `tsv`, and `ndjson`.

As a library's size doesn't matter much in Python (compared to say JS), I'm inclined in supporting all formats by default, but before would like to know if this caused any issues before.

cc @roll

`pip install keystoneauth1` effectively resolved the issue.  Why is this not installed by keystone or one of our packages already?  *spooky music*

Any chance we could get get `nose` and `mock` moved into `tests_require` in `setup.py` so we don't have them in production? 

Thanks!

issue_title: tests & provisioning should wait for full etcd quorum
issue_body:
Run `make dev-cluster && deisctl list`. Notice that most services were scheduled to a single node. This is because fleet responded while etcd was still growing the cluster, so services were scheduled while only one machine was a member. Additionally, some AWS tests have stalled out here:
```
Routing mesh...
deis-router@3.service: loaded
deis-router@2.service: loaded
```
My assumption is that we don't yet have 3 machines in the cluster at this point.

Really all provisioning and tests of this type should poll until `fleetctl list-machines -no-legend | wc -l` equals `DEIS_NUM_INSTANCES` so that units are scheduled properly.

Anyway - all the tutorials states that i need to, more or less, make the follwing (i've skipped the rest for simplicity's sake)

- generate a new ssh key `~/.ssh/deis(.pub)`
- add a content of `~/.ssh/deis` to `parameters.json` to `sshKeyData` value.
- run `azure group create (...)`
- add deis key `ssh-add -K ~/.ssh/deis`

However, for some reason, azure wont let me put contents of `~/.ssh/deis` as sshKeyData (i've removed the beginning and end lines and all the new-lines from it). When i try to run `azure group create` with `deis` key in `parameters.json`, i can see in the management portal that provisioning has failed because:

{"error":{"code":"InvalidParameter","target":"linuxConfiguration.ssh.publicKeys.keyData","message":"The value of parameter linuxConfiguration.ssh.publicKeys.keyData is invalid."}}

I'm able to finalize a provisioning by providing `deis.pub` instead of `deis` contents but it seems not to be the way to go and it breaks later anyway so i'm not gonna write about that (or should i?).

my `sshKeyData` looks like that:

```
"sshKeyData": {
    "value": "(...)"
  },
```

Thanks in advance for any clues! I'm really excited to use deis and i hope that might be resolved :)
Mike

issue_title: Keep column names instead of renaming to Source and Destination.
Fix CI to skip slow tests.
issue_body:
### What is wrong?

The slowest tests are marked here:

https://github.com/ethereum/py-evm/blob/master/tests/json-fixtures/test_state.py#L62-L113

* These tests **should not** be executed in CI runs initiated by pull requests.
* These tests **should** execute in the daily cron test run.

The test suite uses the `TRAVIS_EVENT_TYPE` to determine if it is running in the `cron` context.

### How can it be fixed

Investigate why this isn't behaving as expected since it looks like the Byzantium test suite is running long when it should be around 5-10 minutes runtime.

issue_title: [4.0.0 beta] No callback ability for events like in the 3.x version?
issue_body:
Playing around with the web3py 4.0.0 beta to get familiar with the new library for when its eventually fully released.

I noticed that in the previous version when listening for events using `.watch(callback_function_name)` you would be able to trigger a particular function when a new event shows up. Looking through the documentation for the new version there doesn't appear to be a similar method. 

As such I'm using the following pattern to listen for new events (if there's a better pattern I would love to find out)

```
while True:
    events = eventFilterInstance.get_new_entries()
    if len(events) > 0:
        # do stuff
```

This does work,  but it seems a little awkward.

When used on osx, the ctypes find_library() command does not find dependency libraries (probably because it does not search anaconda lib directories - seems to work fine on linux).  This can be worked around by setting DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH.  A similar issue was discussed in ticket [#308](https://github.com/conda/conda/issues/308#issuecomment-26323101), with the final decision being that the underlying issues in the library (ZMQ::FFI) should be fixed rather than depending on DYLD_LIBRARY_PATH.  What is the suggested solution for building conda distributions on osx for libraries that depend on ctypes.find_library()?

issue_title: conda env update fails with pip dependencies if Python version changes
issue_body:
**I'm submitting a...** - [x] bug report - [ ] feature request ## Current Behavior `conda env update -n -f environment.yml` fails if both of these conditions are met: 1. The Python version changes 2. There is at least one dependency to be installed with pip The error looks like: ``` Unable to install package for pip. Please double check and ensure you dependencies file has the correct spelling. You might also try installing the conda-env-pip package to see if provides the required installer. ``` This is the same error but not the same bug as #4985. pip is installed and specified as a dependency, but switching Python version in the same transaction results in conda failing to find pip. ### Steps to Reproduce Create an environment.yml that contains Python and : ```yaml # environment.yml dependencies: - python=3.5 - pip - pip: - wheel ``` Create an environment with a *different* Python: ``` conda create -n test python=3.6 pip ``` and then try to update it with the environment file ``` conda env update -n test -f environment.yml ``` ``` ``` ## Expected Behavior `conda env update` should succeed. ## Environment Information Run in docker with [these files](https://gist.github.com/7d234cbcf69df2c7cd68523c9327db71).
`conda info`

``` active environment : None user config file : /root/.condarc populated config files : conda version : 4.4.8 conda-build version : not installed python version : 3.6.2.final.0 base environment : /opt/conda (writable) channel URLs : https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/main/noarch https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/free/noarch https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/r/noarch https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/linux-64 https://repo.continuum.io/pkgs/pro/noarch package cache : /opt/conda/pkgs /root/.conda/pkgs envs directories : /opt/conda/envs /root/.conda/envs platform : linux-64 user-agent : conda/4.4.8 requests/2.18.4 CPython/3.6.2 Linux/4.9.75-linuxkit-aufs debian/8 glibc/2.19 UID:GID : 0:0 netrc file : None offline mode : False ```

issue_title: Local Conda installation failing due to permissions on shared hosting
issue_body:
I can't seem to get any help with this on  [stackoverflow](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/45018272/local-conda-installation-failing-due-to-permissions-on-shared-hosting) or the [google group](https://groups.google.com/a/continuum.io/forum/#!topic/conda/IJg6etdzM3c).

I had thought I could create an isolated, local installation. But it turns out you seem to need links to system packages belonging to root. I think this is the essence of [this question](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/41640056/do-conda-environments-have-access-to-root-environment-system-packages), which remains unanswered.

I've been using conda quite a bit with non-root installs on servers. Right now, I'm trying to install it on a Bluehost shared server. They have recently changed the ssh shell to a jailshell that does not allow linking to system packages. I did not realize this would be an obstacle. However...

issue_title: Add support for 'OptionalParameters' to group deployment create|validate
issue_body:
There needs to be a way to pass optional parameters to a group deployment, while at the same time passing in `--template-file <path tot template file>` and `--parameters @<path to template parameters file>`.

The Azure SDK tools build on the notion of passing in `_artifactsLocation` and `_artifactsLocationSasToken` parameters in the template file you would pass in the` --template-file` parameter for az group deployment create|deploy.  The precedent that has been set is for these values to be configured dynamically, which the Azure SDK team has done elegantly in the PowerShell script they produce as part of an Azure Resource Group project in Visual Studio. 

To deploy templates that have been developed using the Azure SDK tools, support for an array of optional parameters is needed.  For example,

`az group deployment create --resource-group $RSRC_GRP_NAME --template-file $TEMPLATE_FILE --parameters "@${TEMPLATE_PARAMETERS_FILE}" ${optional-parameters}`

issue_title: [NSG] Creating new rules seems a little too verbose
issue_body:
This is more of an observation issue, but I've noticed that when using the `az network nsg rule create` command, it feels a little overly verbose to do simple things.  For example, to enable incoming SSH, you need to run the following:

```shell
az network nsg rule create -g <GROUP> \
  -n <NAME> \
  --access allow \
  --destination-address-prefix '*' \
  --destination-port-range 22 \
  --direction inbound \
  --nsg-name <NSG> \
  --protocol tcp \
  --source-address-prefix '*' \
  --source-port-range '*' \
  --priority 1000
```

The `az vm create` command simplifies this by auto-enabling SSH for you (by default), but ideally, the underlying commands for managing NSG rules would be as simplified as possible as well, since many users might want to manage their NSG rules as part of a VNet, instead of relying on the `az vm create` command to handle that at the VM level.

I'm not sure what the best solution is here, but it would be great to determine whether any of the currently required parameters could have sensible defaults given to them and/or if the CLI could provide a "service" concept like the portal has, where you can add rules for well known protocols, which would in turn provide even more sensible defaults to the above (e.g. it would know that SSH uses port 22 by default).

> Note: Inversely, the `--priority` flag does in fact provide a default value, but its a "static value" (it's always `1000`), and therefore, you can only rely on the default value for the first rule you add to an NSG, or else you'll receive a conflict. This behavior is kind of odd (at least to me), and so, I've begun to not rely on that default.

issue_title: Clients need to handle refresh token if supplied during client construction
issue_body:
I mentioned this in our slack conversation last week, adding it here to keep it tracked.
We need to ensure that we maintain the same level of support and functionality that exist in the AutoRest clients. In those clients there is a base class called ServiceClient which is ultimately responsible for all http requests being sent and received. This class has logic that handles token expiration and attempts to refresh the token given the credentials that were initially used during client construction. Our client must also do this to ensure that we don't end up with expired tokens during long running operations (such as file upload/download).

Here is a link to the specific logic within AutoRest that handles the refresh: https://github.com/Azure/autorest/blob/45ddc1a84c58f5732a2f77418079c9439f39e892/src/client/Python/msrest/msrest/service_client.py#L189

The basic algorithm is:
1. attempt to send request
2. catch invalidgrant and token expired errors
3. on those errors trigger a token refresh and resend the request
4. If the request still fails due to invalidgrant or tokenexpired then we raise a token expired error.

## Long story short
[Chardet 3.0 was released today](https://pypi.python.org/pypi/chardet). Aiohttp refers to chardet without a version boundary and so all new installs of aiohttp install Chardet 3.0. This is causing issues in aiohttp. This got caught by our test suite: https://travis-ci.org/home-assistant/home-assistant/jobs/221068366#L285

Aiohttp experienced similar problems with Yarl in the past. The solution for that was then to pin Yarl to a specific range.

## Expected behaviour

Short term: Chardet dependency to be limited to <3.

## Actual behaviour

Chardet 3.0 is getting installed.

## Steps to reproduce

```
git clone https://github.com/home-assistant/home-assistant.git
cd home-assistant
python3 -m venv .
source bin/activate
pip3 install tox
tox
```

## Your environment

Tests ran on Travis CI. Test failed on Python 3.4, 3.5, 3.6 and 3.6-dev.

## Workaround

Install `chardet==2.3` before installing aiohttp

    if len(dp) > 1:

[PEP-561](https://www.python.org/dev/peps/pep-0561/) enables package maintainers to distribute their typing stub files and have mypy (as of version 0.590) use them from the installed package. Since multidict already includes stub files, the only thing that needs to be done is to distribute a file named `py.typed` in the package directory.

Hi,
Do you all have any interest in adding type hints to schematics?  I find adding them sort of meditative so I'd be glad to work on it for this project.  If you're interested, you have two choices for how to go about it:

## option 1: stubs
create .pyi stub files that describe the types of arguments of all public functions in the library.  these reside alongside their .py partners and are used by apps like PyCharm and mypy to give errors and completions when `schematics` is used within other projects.

### pros
- does not impact existing code at all
- does not require adding `typing` module as a requirement

### cons
- stubs have to be manually updated any time methods are added, removed, renamed, or the signature changes

## option 2: annotations
serves the same purpose as .pyi files, but the annotations live in the code instead of separate files.

### pros
- only one set of files to maintain
- good starting point for adding complete static type checking to schematics.  stubs are only useful for projects that *consume* `schematics`.  annotations used within `schematics` code could be used to statically type check `schematics` itself as part of the CI process.

### cons
- [comment-based type annotations](http://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/python2.html) are fairly ugly.  you have to go to 3.5+ to get syntactical support.  I'm working on tools to convert comment-based type annotations to 3.5-compatible code, so when the project does eventually go 3.x-only you won't have to refactor it all by hand.
- requires adding `typing` module as a requirement for python 2.x
- may require inheriting from [`typing.Generic`](http://mypy.readthedocs.io/en/latest/generics.html#defining-generic-classes) in a few cases instead of `object`.

issue_title: Exporting ApplicationName as an AWS tag for ELBs
issue_body:
We need a way to identify ELBs of multiple instances of the same application (i.e. multiple instances of `LizzyAgent` hosted in different AWS accounts).

I added a way to do this by setting `ApplicationName` attribute in `SenzaInfo` section:
```
# basic information for generating and executing this definition
SenzaInfo:
  StackName: hello-<team_name>
  ApplicationName: hello-app
```
which will be exported as an AWS tag for the ELB.

NMAKE : fatal error U1077: '"C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft Visual Studio 14.0\VC\BIN\cl.EXE"' : return code '0x2'
```

I think the right fix is to define this flag before including windows.h:
```
#define NOWINBASEINTERLOCK
#include <windows.h>
```

issue_title: Add the ability to set a `column_group` on a column
issue_body:
- [ ]  Update renderer 
- [ ] Get columns set up

Slack reference
```python
class SentTimeGroup(ColumnGroup):
	label = 'Sent Time (s)
class RacesGrid(Grid):
    RaceIdColumn('Id', ents.Race.id)
    Column('Max', some_aggregate, filters.NumericFilter, group=SentTimeGroup)
```

> Randy Syring [1:56 PM]
There is a lot of wasted space in the header cells b/c of the need to repeate "Sent Time (s)" and "Receive Time (s)" in columns.
What if we created the ability to set a `column_group` on a column so that the grid looked like: [example](https://files.slack.com/files-pri/T02DAMQ4M-F93F8JS4Q/image.png)

> Matt Lewellyn [2:00 PM]
adding that to webgrid or rolling it into your project would be fairly trivial, I think

> Bill Adams [2:01 PM]
That would require updates to the renderers. I'm not sure how difficult it would be to get that property passed through on the column to the renderer
It could possibly be done without any updates to webgrid


> Randy Syring [2:02 PM]
My thinking was to roll it into webgrid.

> Matt Lewellyn [2:03 PM]
I agree it might be useful. The renderer piece of it should be the trivial part. Could be more of a task to get the columns set up, with the way webgrid creates those for the grid instance

issue_title: Original slug field language changes depending on editor's language
issue_body:
Hi there,

I've noticed this issue when, by accident, I was using Wagtail admin's interface in the non-default language of my website and later noticed that my `{% slugurl 'slug_value' %}` statements ceased to work. Steps to reproduce this issue:

### 1. Using wagtail's admin interface in English

Save a page with `slug_en='english'` and `slug_en='portuguese'`.

Any declarations of `{% slugurl 'english' %}` will work in either language. In the database   `wagtailcore_page.slug` should read `'english'`.

### 2.  Using wagtail's admin interface in a different language (Portuguese for example)

Save the same page with exactly the same slugs (`slug_en='english'` and `slug_en='portuguese'`).

Declarations of `{% slugurl 'english' %}` break in both languages and `wagtailcore_page.slug` reads `'portuguese'`.


The behaviour above is consistent with `django-modeltranslation`'s [State of the Original Field](http://django-modeltranslation.readthedocs.io/en/latest/usage.html#the-state-of-the-original-field) but if editors of different languages are using/translating a wagtail website they will likely break any links that use  `{% slugurl 'slug_value' %}`.

I suggest `wagtail-modeltranslation` works around this `django-modeltranslation` limitation and enforces a consistent slug value in the DB.

issue_title: Any idea on how to optimize time and memory usage for angr?
issue_body:

Please mark this issue as question.

issue_body:
I am using wechatpy to add wechat login capability to a website, and I found that WeChat's servers refuse to accept the URLs generated by WeChatOAuth.qrconnect_url.

After some investigation, I found that this method does not encode URLs correctly.

WeChat servers will accept the URLs after changing:

        redirect_uri = quote(self.redirect_uri)

to

        redirect_uri = quote(self.redirect_uri, safe=u'')

Wanted to pass this along to the team and other users who are unable to present their users with the login QR code.
-gary

I'm starting to think about being able to use the `ganga` module to run tests (at least some of them). For this purpose we need to be sure exactly what it should be providing or doing. As far as I can tell there are 3 (or 5) main tasks that it needs to do and which we should account for in little ganga. They are (in no particular order):

1. Load all plugin objects into the GPI as well as things like `export` and `report`.
2. Start the registry and the repository so that things which expect them to be present can interact with them (e.g. creating a proxied `Job` object adds itself to the registry)
3. Start background subsystems like the queues thread pool and the monitoring loop
4. Load external packages using each package's `RuntimePackage` instance (running their `BOOT.py`, calling `loadPlugins` etc.)
5. Registering shutdown hooks

Now, number 4 is almost just a repeat of number 1 for each package so conceptually they are the same. Number 5 I feel could be handled automatically during 2 and 3 so that each subsystem automatically registers its own shutdown handlers (it's not a priority to change this though).

What I would like is that a basic `import ganga` only performs something like 2, 4, 1, 5 by default and the monitoring loop should not start automatically. A function to start and stop the monitoring loop should be provided. Perhaps the code that is run during number 5 should also be provided so that the user can shut down all runtime objects (reg, repo etc.).

What are people's thoughts on this? Am I missing any obvious requirements? How does it sound from a user's point of view?

In Ganga we have a system called 'filter' ([Filters.py](https://github.com/ganga-devs/ganga/blob/develop/python/Ganga/GPIDev/Base/Filters.py)) which seem to be a way of injecting code before assignments happen on schema items.

For example, setting
```python
j.postprocessors = FileChecker()
```
will have a filter (defined in IPostProcessors.py) intercede, and wrap the `FileChecker` object in a `MultiPostProcessor` so that doing
```python
type(j.postprocessors)
```
gives `Ganga.GPIDev.Base.Proxy.MultiPostProcessor`.

This is work which would make so much more sense being implemented in a standard Python `@property` 'setter' inside the `Job` class.

Apart from that, filters seem to provide the possibility of doing
```python
j.backend = 'Remote'
```
and have it look up the plugin automatically but I'm unsure that anyone uses this in reality.

In summary, I feel that the filter system is too magical and scatters the code that is doing the interception throughout the code base. We need to consider whether the feature is worth the complexity and perhaps consider it for removal in the future.

I've heard many Crawlera users complaining about installing the certificate (to make https work with Crawlera) and I was thinking that, since the Crawlera middleware is meant to make things easier on the client side, it could automatically download and install the certificate if it's not present, to reduce another step in the "crawlera setup process". Installing the certificate is not a major task, but also not a trivial one, and it varies per platform. But most importantly, it can be automated in things like the Crawlera middleware.

issue_title: Potential security issues with `get_ip()` changes in v4
issue_body:
I'm a little concerned by the change to use `ipware.ip.get_ip()` in v4.

1. There is no documentation to suggest that `django-ipware` may need to be configured.
2. There is now no documentation on the potential attack vector if the proxy doesn't set `X-Forwarded-For` (or whatever the first header used in `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` is) explicitly, c.f. [`USE_X_FORWARDED_HOST`](https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/2.0/ref/settings/#std:setting-USE_X_FORWARDED_HOST)
3. ~~`django-ipware` says to use `get_ip()` if the server is **not** accessible on the Internet, see [here](https://github.com/un33k/django-ipware#how-to-use).~~ (Although looking at the code the distinction between `get_ip()` and `get_real_ip()`, the latter doesn't count private IP addresses as "real". What they really mean here is use `get_ip()` if there are also clients that may connect from an internal private network otherwise use `get_real_ip()`.)
4. Previously `django-axes` allowed configuring the maximum number of proxies in case of spoofed entries. This is no longer possible. `django-ipware` provides `get_trusted_ip()` but it isn't particularly useful in it's current form.

It would probably be wise to recommend setting `IPWARE_META_PRECEDENCE_ORDER` to `['HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR', 'REMOTE_ADDR']` or at least indicate that this was the original behaviour. Checking all of these headers by default feels risky.

Forgive me, but this feels rushed and ill thought through. It was working fine for me and issue #275 and pull request #280 fail to even give any evidence of a problem. Every comment in #275 is regarding `AttributeError` when looking up `META` on `NoneType` - `django-ipware` is still using `request.META`. This feels like a push to remove code in favour of a dependency for minor gain.

issue_title: Don't remove older versions from PyPI
issue_body: When using [pip-tools](https://github.com/jazzband/pip-tools) to pin requirements, you get `django-embed-video==1.1.0` (for example). `1.1.0` appears to have been removed from PyPI. Since `1.1.2` doesn't fix any security vulnerabilities or make `1.1.0` otherwise dangerous to use, it would be beneficial to keep the old versions on PyPI for pinned version builds to stay reproducible.

Currently on each dashboard:

- An "all tags" section is visible, i.e. "Tags matching: /.*/"
- Tag sections are collapsed
- Entering a search causes the "all tags" section to drill down
- Clearing the search box causes the "all tags" section to disappear

We've received feedback from multiple individuals that the first thing they do in their workflows is collapse the first section and open the other ones.

It might be better to change the UI to the following:

1. Remove "all tags" section
2. Expand all sections by default (should be possible, thanks to pagination)
3. Make search bar invisible by default (free up vertical whitespace: content is king)
4. Add magnifying glass icon (left to refresh button) that toggles visibility of search bar
5. When initially clicked, search bar initializes to empty (defined as `.*`) and "all tags" section appears
6. Typing search term causes the "Tags matching" section to update in real time, as it does now
7. *Maybe* put "x" button to right of search bar that does same thing as clicking search button in top right corner a second time (de-toggling)
8. Ensure that all these search states persist to the fragment section of the URL bar

Thoughts @wchargin @chihuahua @dandelionmane?

issue_title: Commissioner/Joiner Router port usage is incorrect in Joiner Authentication
issue_body:
According to the the 1.1 spec, during the Joiner Authentication process, the Joiner Router should send to the Border Agent from an ephemeral port to port MM (61631), and should receive replies from the Border Agent on this ephemeral port.

joiner_router.cpp is not listening on this ephemeral port after sending the RLY_RX. Further, the Commissioner is then processing the DTLS-ClientHello and sending the RLY_TX to a hard-coded port (MM, 61631), where the Joiner Router is listening (which keeps things working in OpenThread, but is incompatible with the spec).

So there are two parts to this issue:
1) The joiner router should be listening/expecting to RLY_TX reply on whatever port it sends from rather than only on 61631. My (and I assume other 3rd party) off mesh border agent/commissioner which don't send to port 61631 end up going through the generic HandleUdpReceive path which breaks the joiner authentication.
2) The commissioner/border agent (these two roles seem to be treated the same in the codebase) should not be sending to a hard coded port 61631. A joiner router that is waiting for the RLY_TX on the port it sent from would not expect this port to be used for the reply, so OpenThread would be incompatible with third party Joiner Routers.

issue_title: numpy array wrappers providing STL compliant interface
issue_body:
Hi Wenzel,

I am currently developing a python extension module that does heavy manipulations on numpy arrays. Moreover, since this module can also be used by other C++ modules that have no relation with Python, the code must be generic enough to work with both numpy arrays and STL containers. For that, I've developed a wrapper around pybind11::array_t that provides a STL compliant interface (it currently works for 1D arrays only, but this will be enhanced in the future)

I think this code could be of interest for other people, but it should be in a dedicated module depending on pybind11 in order to have a good segregation of duty.

Moreover, since performance is critical, I want to avoid many calls to the Python C API, and that would require a few changes in pybind11 (one of these changes would be to store the Py_buffer inside the buffer_info as a shared_ptr instead of a raw pointer).

So my questions:
- I foresee that this would probably be a rather large amout of code. Would you consider this functionality to be in scope for pybind project ?
- At which point would it make sense for it to be in a different repo for the  pybind organization ?
- If not, would you be ok with the few changes in pybind11 mentioned earlier to enable me to have my wrappers working in a separate project ?
Is there any way to retrieve operational attributes (as '+' in `ldapsearch`)? I need to get `nsAccountLock` to check if user acount is active or not.

issue_title: Filters are not preserved in polymorphic parent admin
issue_body:
Inheriting from PolymorphicParentModelAdmin class break the django admin "preserve filter" behavior. Removing the "get_preserved_filters" in polymorphic/admin/parentadmin.py solves the problem.
Why is this override needed?

issue_title: modelformset_factory() got an unexpected keyword argument 'fk_name'
issue_body:
In `polymorphic.formsets.polymorphic_modelformset_factory`, we have
```
    kwargs = {
        ...
        'fk_name': fk_name,
        ...
    }
    FormSet = modelformset_factory(**kwargs)
```
while django's `modelformset_factory` does not have a `fk_name` argument, at least in the recent versions. Therefore I'm getting `modelformset_factory() got an unexpected keyword argument 'fk_name'`
See
https://docs.djangoproject.com/en/1.10/ref/forms/models/#modelformset-factory

issue_title: Improve error message for external tasks
issue_body:
I find the error prompt here is not ideal:

```
ERROR: 1a3273c5b127f0cb34aa5e2ac62f9d58 failed with RuntimeError error Failed to execute script (ret=1).
Please use command
	Rscript \
	  --default-packages=datasets,methods,utils,stats,grDevices,graphics \
	  /home/gaow/tmp/test/.sos/core_fl_0_16_e3e26db7.R
under "/home/gaow/tmp/test" to test it.
```

For this example the computation is quite heavy. It takes up to an hour to reproduce the error. When executed as non-task the error message would dump on the screen, which is ugly but can give people some hints. 

However here apparently one can see the error message via `sos status 1a3273c5b127f0cb34aa5e2ac62f9d58 -v 4`. That opens the err file which has more information including the prompt above. That can save people from re-running. I'm wondering if it is possible to distinguish between task/non-task case and ask users to use `sos status` to check the job, instead of suggesting a rerun.

Suppose someone sets `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=4,5,6` and then starts Ray on a machine with `num_gpus=3`. Right now, `ray.get_gpu_ids()` will return values in `[0, 1, 2]`. Instead, it should return values in `[4, 5, 6]`.

Two more cases:
1. If `num_gpus = 4` and `CUDA_VISIBLE_DEVICES=1,2,3`, then raise an exception.

Right now there are some hiccups when using custom preprocessor:

- register_preprocessor doesn't work due to to a small bug (PR underway)

issue_title: Calling a remote function that creates tensorflow variables crashes in some cases
issue_body:
### System information
- **OS Platform and Distribution (e.g., Linux Ubuntu 16.04)**: Ubuntu 16.04.3 LTS
- **Ray installed from (source or binary)**: pip install
- **Ray version**: 0.4.0
- **Gym version**: 0.9.5
- **Python version**: 3.5.4
- **Exact command to reproduce**: see source code

### Describe the problem
There is seemingly inconsistent behavior in how tensorflow and remote tasks interact. When creating multiple tensor-flow graphs from the model catalog on the same device, there is a name conflict which causes a crash. I tried a variety of setups and got the following results:

- If you create multiple graphs with each one in a different actor everything is fine
- If you create multiple graphs in the same actor it causes a crash
- If you create multiple graphs with each one in a different remote function everything is fine with a small number of remote functions
- **Bug**: If you create multiple graphs with each one in a different remote function with a large number of remote functions then it causes a crash
- If you create a graph within an agent, then use a member function to create a second graph within that agent it causes a crash. 
- **Bug**: If you create a graph within an agent, then a member function of that agent calls a remote function that create a graph it causes a crash. This is unexpected to me, I thought the behavior of a remote function launched from the driver should be the same as a remote function launched from an actor.

issue_title: Process widget comm messages in the shell channel when Kernel's main thread is busy
issue_body:
I am trying to implement a widget to integrate the monitoring and visualization of Apache Spark jobs from within the notebook, including displaying of cell level progress bars and job cancel buttons. Apache Spark blocks the kernel thread and waits until the job is finished. The widget that monitors this job needs to communicate with the kernel while it is busy.

Currently from a background thread, the kernel side component is able to send comm messages via `IOPub` channel to the front-end widget. `IOPub` runs on its own thread and seems to be thread safe to publish messages from a background thread. So the kernel side can send progress updates.

However Widget Comm messages sent from the front-end to the kernel, are processed in the `shell` channel which is flushed only in the main thread loop of the kernel. When the kernel is busy executing, widgets can no longer send messages to the kernel, ie. kernel cannot process these messages until its main thread is free. This prevents the implementation of a stop button, or polling for updates(as mentioned in #1287). 
(please correct me if i am wrong)

I have tried using `ipython.kernel.do_one_iteration()` from a background thread for the incoming messages to get flushed, but this triggers all the shell channel messages to get processed including execution requests of later cells, which are not supposed to be processed until the current cell is done. This breaks the flow of the notebook.

I have gone through #642, but I cannot put the execution of spark in a separate thread as the widget has to be seamlessly integrated with the notebook. Would require asking the user to run the job on another thread.

Is it possible to create a mechanism to selectively process incoming widget comm messages from `shell_stream` in a background thread in the kernel, so that widgets can remain responsive while the kernel is busy?

Or it may be required to create a separate channel for full duplex communication with widgets from a background thread, How can I do this?

I'm running it on Kali Linux. Any ideas why it isn't working?

First off thank you for all your hard work, I'm having issues getting the script to run after installation on my RPi3 using Raspbian (Jessie) when I run the command in terminal:

pi@databass:~ $ cd instabot/examples
pi@databass:~/instabot/examples $ python multi_script_CLI.py

ImportError: No module named _vendor

Everything appeared to install correctly, I have both python 2 & 3 installed on my system.

This is command line help for `datacube dataset update`

```
Usage: datacube dataset update [OPTIONS] [DATASETS]...

  Update datasets in the Data Cube

Options:
  --allow-any TEXT        Allow any changes to the specified key (a.b.c)
  -r, --match-rules PATH  Rules to be used to associate datasets with products
  -t, --dtype TEXT        Product to be associated with the datasets
  -a, --auto-match        Automatically associate datasets with products by
                          matching metadata
  --dry-run               Check if everything is ok
  -h, --help              Show this message and exit.
```

Notice how it has all the **auto-matching** rules that `datacube dataset add` has. Why are they needed? I assume that I can only update dataset that already exists, if it already exists it is already associated with some product, surely changing product of an already indexed dataset shouldn't be allowed? Or is `update` also implies possibly `add` if missing?

This brings us to the other important question:

- Exactly what aspects of dataset are safe to change?

Neither command line help, nor `readthedocs` give any indication as to what aspects of Dataset are meant to be immutable and which can be safely changed. One obvious thing that can be safely changed is dataset location on disk. Probably various bits of metadata that are meant for human consumption can also be changed (correcting a typo in a description field, that sort of thing).

In general dataset management beyond addition is fairly opaque as far as documentation goes.

issue_title: json resource runtime error: dictionary changed size during iteration
issue_body:
hello @aranega 

I'm getting the error: 
```
  File "/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pyecore/resources/resource.py", line 84, in get_resource
    resource.load(options=options)
  File "/env/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pyecore/resources/json.py", line 35, in load
    for inst, refs in self._load_href.items():
RuntimeError: dictionary changed size during iteration
```
From what I can tell, this only happens in Python 3.x because of how Python 3 iterates through a dictionary (by iterator instead of making a copy of the keys). 

From what I can tell, the offending code is at line 166
```
self._load_href[inst] = ereferences
```
which is part of the function `to_obj` which can be called in the function `process_inst` which is called for each iteration over self._load_href:

```python
for inst, refs in self._load_href.items():
    self.process_inst(inst, refs)
```

I've changed the iteration to the following (and it appears to be working):

```python
for inst in list(self._load_href.keys()):
    refs = self._load_href[inst]
    self.process_inst(inst, refs)
```

However, I don't fully understand the process here so this might be a bad fix. 

Thanks,

issue_title: Tornado >=4.5 support still broken in some cases
issue_body: auto tornado assumes "handler_class" and "regex" properties on the "spec" objects, consistent with the URLSpec object. However, in Tornado 4.5, routes may be rules (and URLSpec is a subclass of rule), and rules have "target" and "matcher" properties (with regex @ matcher.regex).

issue_title: No documentation for the FileID.size attribute appears on ReadTheDocs
issue_body:
I have a job that needs to use some files from the file store. I initially tried with with just 1 GB of disk, but that's not enough to download the files that it wants to work with.

I'm trying to rewrite the job to measure how big the files it wants to use are, and to re-queue itself with the appropriate amount of disk required, so it can actually download them. But the file store doesn't seem to expose a file size query. All you can do with a file in the file store is read it---and I think even if you read it streaming, your job is liable to fail if the file is bigger than the amount of disk available on the node you are running on.

The file store needs to expose file sizes, so that jobs can know how much disk they need to ask for in order to use particular files.

issue_title: next actions cannot distinguish between parent model and the original model
issue_body:
if actions are connected to 2 models, `initial` and `followup` where `followup` has ` FK to `initial`, the `followup` model eventually cannot be created because the `FollowupAction` does not distinguish between the parent (either initial or followup) and the original action (initial). that is, a followup can spawn a new followup so in the case:

initial -> followup(1) -> followup(2) ->followup(3)

followup(1) creates OK since the parent (initial) is the same as the FK
followup(2) fails since the parent (followup) is not the FK (initial)

Need to separate the concepts of parent, which may be `initial` or `followup`, and the FK, which is always `initial`

j...@pobox.com, 2010-01-10T00:26:29.000Z 
rdflib's setup.py uses setuptools.find_packages, which finds module test in 
addition to modules rdflib and rdflib_tools.  As a result, the egg info 
installed by setuptools lists module test -- e.g. on my Ubuntu karmic 
system:

$ cat /usr/lib/pymodules/python2.6/rdflib-2.4.0.egg-info/top_level.txt
test
rdflib
rdflib_tools

For some reason I haven't investigated, module test doesn't actually appear 
to get installed.  That's a good thing, because Python itself comes with a 
module named "test".

However, the presence of "test" in top_level.txt causes spurious warnings 
when working on any other project on the same system that both 1. imports 
pkg_resources (which may happen indirectly, for example by importing a 
setuptools "namespace package" such as zope.interface) and 2. has a module 
named "test" -- for example, a file named test.py that runs that project's 
tests.

The solution would be to stop using find_packages and just list all of the 
packages in rdflib explicitly (rdflib has exactly two packages, after all -
- hardly a great maintenance burden):

sed -i -e 's/find_packages()/["rdflib", "rdflib_tools"]/' setup.py


What steps will reproduce the problem?

$ cat <<"EOF" > fail.sh
sudo apt-get install python-rdflib
dir=$(mktemp -d)
echo "$dir"
cd "$dir"
touch test.py
python -c "import test; import rdflib"
EOF
$ sh fail.sh

What is the expected output? What do you see instead?

Expect: No warning

Got:
...
/usr/lib/python2.6/dist-packages/zope/__init__.py:19: UserWarning: Module 
test was already imported from test.py, but /usr/lib/pymodules/python2.6 is 
being added to sys.path
  import pkg_resources


What version of the product are you using? On what operating system?

python-rdflib 2.4.0-5ubuntu1 Ubuntu 9.10 (karmic)
